abstract:
Higher education is a fundamental driver of socio-economic development, knowledge diffusion, and national competitiveness. While Ethiopia has shifted from an elitist educational model to one characterized by rapid massification over the last two decades, this expansion has raised critical concerns regarding the quality of educational services. This study sought to evaluate the persistent challenges facing Ethiopian higher education and examine the efficacy of government-led quality improvement initiatives. Adopting a qualitative research design, the study utilized document analysis, interviews, and focus group discussions. A comprehensive systematic review was conducted on 89 sources, including legislative policy documents, peer-reviewed journals, doctoral dissertations, and empirical studies spanning from 2004 to 2021. 

The findings indicate that despite the establishment of regulatory bodies such as the Higher Education Relevance and Quality Agency (HERQA) and the implementation of various reform programs, these efforts have had a negligible impact on actual institutional quality. Primary obstacles include a severe shortage of experienced academic staff, inadequate Information Communication Technology (ICT) infrastructure, and a lack of essential physical resources such as laboratories, workshops, libraries, and classrooms. Furthermore, student welfare is hindered by substandard dormitory and administrative services. The study concludes that the misalignment between breakneck expansion and resource allocation has resulted in poor service delivery across most public universities. To address these systemic gaps, it is recommended that the Ethiopian government prioritize human and material resource development, strengthen internal quality assurance mechanisms, and conduct rigorous institutional readiness assessments before further expanding the higher education sector.
